coredata.dc:title: Design and selective photocatalytic activity of highly concentrated C, N, O co-doped Zn, S co-defective ZnS particles mediated by ethylenediamine derivatives

coredata.dc:description: ZnS particles are synthesized by adding ethylenediamine (en) and its derivatives, triethylenetetramine (trien) and ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), as capping agents. In the capped ZnS particles, Zn and S defects due to O, N-doping and loading of carbon dots (CDs) are confirmed. The degree of defects is suggested from photoluminescence (PL) spectra and density of states calculations. The intensities of PL are lowered in the order of ZnS-none> ZnS-EDTA> ZnS-en> ZnS-trien, while the photocurrent densities are increased in the opposite order. Time resolved PL shows extremely slow recombination of photo-excited charges in ZnS-trien. ZnS-trien is the most suitable for catalyzing CO2 reduction, and ZnS-en is the best for water-splitting. The excellent photocatalytic activity of ZnS-trien encapsulated with N-CDs shell maintains without photo-corrosion over more than five recycling experiments. Eventually, this study reveals that well-ordered ZnS with C, N, O co-doping-induced Zn, S co-defects is self-assembled using en-derivatives as capping agents, exhibits good photocatalytic activity without grafting with other particles.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ZnS particles are synthesized by adding ethylenediamine (en) and its derivatives, triethylenetetramine (trien) and ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), as capping agents. In the capped ZnS particles, Zn and S defects due to O, N-doping and loading of carbon dots (CDs) are confirmed. The degree of defects is suggested from photoluminescence (PL) spectra and density of states calculations. The intensities of PL are lowered in the order of ZnS-none> ZnS-EDTA> ZnS-en> ZnS-trien, while the photocurrent densities are increased in the opposite order. Time resolved PL shows extremely slow recombination of photo-excited charges in ZnS-trien. ZnS-trien is the most suitable for catalyzing CO2 reduction, and ZnS-en is the best for water-splitting. The excellent photocatalytic activity of ZnS-trien encapsulated with N-CDs shell maintains without photo-corrosion over more than five recycling experiments. Eventually, this study reveals that well-ordered ZnS with C, N, O co-doping-induced Zn, S co-defects is self-assembled using en-derivatives as capping agents, exhibits good photocatalytic activity without grafting with other particles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemical synthesis routes using surfactants to produce well-ordered nanocrystals have been actively researched over the past 20 years. The shape and size of the nanocrystals can be tuned by changing the surfactant concentration. At low concentrations, they experience less capping; thus, the particles can grow randomly into spherical shapes. At optimum surfactant concentration, the nanocrystal is completely capped, yielding uniform cubic nanoparticles [1]. Many studies have reported the syntheses of metal oxide nanoparticles, including ZnO or CuO, using various surfactants. In particular, surfactants, including sodium dodecyl sulfate, trisodium citrate, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, oleic acid, oleic amine, hexadecylamine, polyvinylpyrrolidone, and polyethylene glycol, are used to synthesize well-ordered nanocrystals with regular shapes and controlled sizes [2–6]. These surfactants not only stabilize the crystal plane but also prevent particle aggregation during the synthesis and separation of nanocrystals. For instance, CuO nanocubes were synthesized in the presence of a base using cetyltrimethylammonium bromide as a surfactant and sodium ascorbate as a reducing agent [7]. Nanocrystalline ZnO was grown from the (100) plane using polyvinylpyrrolidone as a surfactant [8]. Reduced metal oxides were synthesized using oleic acid and D-(+)-glucose as surfactants [9]. Uniform cubes, octahedrons, and rhombic dodecahedrons could be obtained by varying the concentration of oleic acid. Many researchers have prepared metal oxides or sulfides in various forms, including nanosheets, nanowires, nanoplates, nanorods, nanoflowers, nanotubes, and nano-hollow structures, using surfactants. However, surfactant molecules that remain in catalytic materials may block reactant molecules from reaching catalytically active sites [10]. Conversely, residual surfactants can decompose and contribute to the formation of hydrogen- or carbon-based products, which may positively affect the photocatalytic activity, product conversion capacity, and selectivity [11].

Herein, ethylenediamine (en) and its derivatives, triethylenetetramine (trien) and ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), are used as capping agents to control the particle shape and optical properties of ZnS. Among semiconductors, ZnS is recognized as a very good photocatalytic material owing to its high photosensitivity, non-toxicity, low cost, and wide bandgap (∼3.6 eV). Nevertheless, ZnS has low photocatalytic efficiency when used alone because of its fast charge-carrier recombination and low charge separation efficiency [12]. The performance of a photocatalyst is highly dependent on particle size, surface area, and defects [13]. Defects can have contradictory effects, with higher or lower defect concentrations being beneficial or harmful; furthermore, depending on the type and location, a defect can enhance or suppress the photocatalytic activity [14]. ZnS can include defects, such as S vacancies (VS), Zn vacancies (VZn), interstitial S atoms (IS), and interstitial Zn atoms (IZn), with different effects depending on the type [15]. Therefore, it is very important to find an efficient and direct method for synthesizing ZnS nanostructures with optimal surface defect compositions to obtain excellent photoactivity.

Some researchers have used the [Zn(acetate)2(amine)x] complex as a precursor for synthesizing ZnO, and an n-type ZnO thin film with a preferred wurtzite (100) plane was successfully prepared from this precursor [16]. High-quality nanomaterials can be synthesized using amine-based chelating molecules as capping agents in nanoparticle synthesis. Other researchers have further enhanced the value of such nanomaterials by using natural bio-chelating materials, including carbohydrates, plant-derived starches, whey, Aloe vera extract, and tectona leaf extract [17]. However, despite being environmentally friendly, these have not received much attention because of their high costs.

Here, we aim to provide a comprehensive understanding of the effects of morphology and defects on the photocatalytic performance of self-assembled ZnS nanoparticles. Therefore, we prepare ZnS using ethylenediamine (en, 2 N)-based chelate molecules (substances in which two or more atoms with lone-paired electron pairs capable of donating electrons exist in one molecule and form a stable ring by combining with metals). Additionally, its derivatives (trien, 4 N; EDTA, 4 O + 2 N), which are cost-effective capping agents owing to their strong bonding with metals. Spherical ZnS nanoparticles with Zn and S defects induced by C, N, O co-doping are prepared using these en-based capping agents. We investigate the crystal structure, crystallite size, and shape of the ZnS nanoparticles, explaining the relationship between their optical properties and defects. Moreover, we systematically observe electron transport in water splitting and CO2 photoreduction under visible-light irradiation.
The synthesis sequences of the defective ZnS particles and details for characterization, catalytic performance evaluation, and computation are presented in the supplementary material.

Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of the ZnS particles made with different capping agents. Three characteristic peaks observed at 2-theta = 28.59, 47.57, and 56.40° for ZnS-none are attributed to the (111), (220), and (311) facets of sphalerite (cubic, F-43 m, ICDD #00–002–0564) [18]. However, because the peak of the (111) facet is broad and not sharp, it is speculated that the (100) and (101) facets of wurtzite (W) are mixed together. The XRD peaks from the profile of ZnS-none could be identified as a wurtzite-10 H structure (ICDD #00–012–0688, Unit cell contains 10 ZnS), similar to the cubic zinc blende structure, with space group P63mc; a = 3.824 Å and c = 31.20 Å; and cell contents 10ZnS. The wurtzite polytypes evidently form a homologous series (2 H, 4 H, 6 H, 8 H, and 10 H), resulting from the growth phenomena based on screw dislocations [19]. In the profiles of the ZnS particles with capping agents, the peak for the cubic (C)(111) facet at the center was sharp, and in the profiles of ZnS-en and ZnS-EDTA, the W(100) and W(101) facets were distinctly observed. This suggests that when a chelating capping agent is used, hexagonal ZnS, which prefers W(100) and W(101) facets to cubic facets, is advantageously synthesized. However, in a perfect hexagonal wurtzite structure, the W(100) and W(101) facets are the main diffraction planes, and the peak intensity should be much stronger than that of the W(002) facet in the center [20]. Here, the center peak predicted by the W(002) facet was sharper than those from the W(100) and W(101) facets. Therefore, it is reasonable to think that the peak of the W(002) facet at the center corresponds to the C(111) facet, rather than the pure W(002) facet. Acharya et al. proposed that chelate molecules are intercalated between cubic and wurtzite ZnS with different stacked structures and are involved in an intermediate transformation step between the two structures [21]. Furthermore, they revealed that during nuclear growth, a (001) facet, which is an AB repeating pattern in wurtzite, accumulates on an ABC repeating pattern, which is the (111) facet in cubic ZnS. During this accumulation, a chelate molecule is interposed between these facets, connecting the two crystal layers. In this case, a stacked twin defect may form between the two crystal layers. Additionally, cubic crystals are stable at low temperatures, while wurtzite crystals can be formed from bulk ZnS only at temperatures above 1020 °C [22]. Therefore, in order to obtain wurtzite crystals, the cubic zinc blende crystal must be treated at 500 ℃ or higher. Therefore, it can be concluded that cubic and hexagonal crystal structures are partially coexisted in the ZnS particles obtained here. Perhaps the coexistence of wurtzite ZnS and cubic ZnS in this study can be suggested in Fig. S2, as following steps; (1) a chelating molecule such as en binds to the (111) plane of the cubic ZnS crystal seed, (2) when cubic ZnS crystals grow, en molecules are intercalated between the crystals, acting as linkages that hold them together, (3) when the cubic ZnS crystals grow by repeatedly stacking (111) planes, en molecules inserted in the middle of the crystals are removed and the lattice is distorted, resulted the crystals are accumulated by AB type stacking, and finally (4) the repeated AB type stacking of cubic ZnS (111) planes forms (001) planes, which correspond to the characteristic diffraction plane of wurtzite ZnS. Consequently, en molecules are inserted into the crystal growth surface, so that the two crystal structures, wurtzite ZnS and cubic ZnS, coexist and grow. At this time, if the cubic ZnS crystals are stacked in an AB type stack, the lattice will be naturally twinned to minimize the angle strain. Therefore, twin faults may appear at the interface where the crystals are stacked toward the (001) plane. The average sizes of crystals of ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA calculated by the Debye–Scherrer equation [23] are 5.78, 11.15, 14.39, and 13.14 nm, respectively; these do not have an exact relationship with the molecular shape of the capping agent. Fig. 1 b shows SEM images of the shapes of the synthesized ZnS particles. ZnS-none consists of irregularly grown spherical particles of 3–5 µm, which are agglomerates of uniform spherical particles of 500 nm; enlargements of these particles show that they are aggregates of uniform spherical nanoparticles of 10 nm or less in size. Moreover, the contact grains grown by the agglomeration of nanoparticles were clearly visible. ZnS-en particles were densely combined with uniform rice grain-like particles of less than 100 nm, and they aggregated to form large spherical particles of about 4 µm. ZnS-trien formed spherical particles of 2–6 µm with very smooth surfaces. A magnification shows that spherical nanoparticles of about 10 nm or less are uniformly and closely connected within these larger particles. In ZnS-EDTA, 50 nm particles gathered in flower-like shapes to form 300 nm particles, which became densely aggregated to form uniform spherical particles of approximately 5 µm. It has been reported that the chelate molecule bonded to the metal atom determines the shape of the particle; we have previously reported that metal complexes combined with en generally form needle-like shapes [24]. Another study reported that tris formed hexagonal plates in metal complex precursors, benzylamine and butylamine formed short needles (or grains of rice), and NH3 formed hexagonal rods [25]. Carboxylate ligands also affect crystal width: monodentate, bidentate, and tridentate ligands form rods, medium to short rods, and plates, respectively [26]. Herein, spherical or elliptical nanoparticles of 10 nm or less were obtained, and these grew three-dimensionally into micrometer-scale spheres. Meanwhile, the TEM image of Fig. S3 shows a large ZnS crystal measuring 4–4.5 µm. ZnS-none with no capping agent showed dense surfaces with rounded undulations of 500 nm, caused by the three-dimensional aggregation of small crystallites. ZnS-en, bearing a capping agent with the 2 N electron donor, and ZnS-trien, with a capping agent having a 4 N electron donor, had needle-like spherical surfaces. The expected formation mechanism for these surface features is proposed in Fig. S4. These needles were connected by a thin film and covered the ZnS surface. The two bonding lines of Zn ions exposed on the surface may have bonded to the N atom at the end of NH2CH2CH2NH2, thus capping the axes. Moreover, the needles in ZnS-trien were thinner and denser than those in ZnS-en, suggesting that the ZnS surface was completely capped by en chains. Finally, the surface of ZnS-EDTA, with a capping agent having two N and four O as electron donors, showed a surface with jagged curves formed by the dense accumulation of small spherical particles. ZnS-EDTA is denser in shape than ZnS-none. However, the aggregated spherical particles in all ZnS samples were too thick to confirm the sizes of the primary nanoparticles.

High-resolution TEM images of ZnS-none, ZnS-en, and ZnS-trien are presented in Fig. 2. C(111) and C(220) facets appear in the HRTEM image of ZnS-none with lattice spacings of 3.12 and 1.91 Å, respectively; these values are consistent with those reported for pure cubic ZnS [27]. In the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, a C(311) facet with a lattice distance of 1.63 Å and a W(210) facet with that of 1.24 Å were observed. It was suggested to be a polycrystalline from the circular bands obtained by connecting the non-uniformly dispersed dots [28]. Intermittent and hazy circular lines appear in the fast Fourier transform (FFT) image as well, that indicating the presence of defects due to ion vacancies [29]. In general, ion vacancies can serve as beneficial charge traps to prevent photoinduced charge recombination and enhance charge transfer. In the high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) image, ZnS-none shows a round and curved surface like that of a golf ball. Considering the difference in ion concentration on the curved surface, it seems that the surfaces of the aggregates of ZnS nanoparticles are not dense. In the elemental mapping images, Zn and S are uniformly dispersed on the ZnS surface. C, N, and O are also observed; it seems that the concentration of O is the highest, followed by N and C, in that order. Because the distribution of O and N is similar to that of S, O and N seem to be doped at the S sites in the lattice. Because more C is detected outside than inside the crystal, it may be loaded as carbon dots (CDs) on the ZnS surface. The HRTEM image of the ZnS-en particles clearly shows the C(111) facet with a lattice interval of 3.12 Å. However, in the SAED pattern, a W(210) facet with a lattice distance of 1.24 Å appears along with the C(111), C(200), and C(311) facets observed in the XRD pattern. As with ZnS-none, hazy and intermittent FFT circular bands are observed, suggesting the presence of ionic defects. In the HAADF image, the band part pressed in the center in the shape of a baseball is clearly observed. Therefore, the surface formed by the aggregation of nanoparticles does not seem to be dense. In the element mapping image, Zn and S are uniformly dispersed on the ZnS surface with less O but more C and N than for ZnS-none. The N and O elements are considered to be doped at S sites because they are distributed evenly throughout the particle. However, ZnS-en may have more CDs loaded on the surface than ZnS-none does, based on the higher concentration of C appearing on the outer surface. Finally, in the HRTEM image of ZnS-trien, only the C(111) facet is predominant, with a spacing of 3.12 Å. In the SAED pattern, C(111), C(200), and C(311) facets appear as in the other two samples, but very little of the W(210) facet is found. Unlike other particles, the FFT image and SAED pattern showed several dots rather than a circular band; therefore, ZnS-trien is considered to be a single crystal [30]. In the HAADF image, a unique shape similar to that of a dandelion flower connected by a needle is observed. In the mapping images, Zn and S are uniformly distributed throughout the particle, and C is uniformly distributed on the particle surface; however, N is distributed with a high concentration inside the particle and a low concentration outside the particle, and O only exists at a low concentration inside the surface. This indicates that N and O are strongly doped at the S sites of ZnS, with particularly heavy N doping. Moreover, based on the larger size of the mapped circles of C and N, the ZnS-trien particle seems to have a wide CN shell surrounding the surface of the core ZnS particle, as a core–shell structure. Consequently, the ZnS particles prepared here differed in doping concentration, but N and O were doped into the ZnS crystal lattice, and CD or N-CD was embedded on the ZnS surface or enveloped the ZnS particle. We attempted to confirm the presence of C by magnifying the HRTEM images, as shown in Fig. S5. In the ZnS-none particle, C was widely distributed in the lower part like a shadow. In the ZnS-en particles, black spots of approximately 1.5–3 nm are observed on the ZnS lattices, indicating CDs. Meanwhile, in the ZnS-trien particles, the spots on the lattices appear to be coke-stuck, and rather than being inserted into the lattice, it is speculated that they exist as occasional CDs on the surface of the ZnS-trien crystal [31].

Fig. 3 a shows the FT-IR spectra of the four types of ZnS particles. As shown in Fig. S6a, assuming the adsorption of the added thiourea, acetate, and capping agents on the ZnS crystal surface, they could not be removed by thermal decomposition during the synthesis reaction and might instead be doped as impurities on the ZnS lattice or surface. Here, we investigated the fragmented functional groups that became pyrolyzed and adsorbed on the ZnS crystals. Similar peak types are observed from the four samples. The sharp and distinct peaks at 3750 and 3600 cm−1 correspond to the O–H stretching modes that appear in water or alcohol, which occur in the molecules of water used as a solvent [32]; these peaks are distinctly observed in the spectra of ZnS-none and ZnS-EDTA. The broad and distinct peak at 3400 cm−1 is attributed to the N–H stretching mode of the aliphatic primary amine, which arises from the two NH2 groups attached to the ends of the thiourea molecule or the en or en-derivatives [33]. The two weak peaks at 2965 and 2896 cm−1 correspond to the N–H and C–H stretching modes, respectively [34]. The peak at 2965 cm−1 in the spectra of ZnS due to the capping agents is stronger owing to the en molecules. The small split peaks at approximately 1644 cm−1, observed in all samples, are related to the CC stretching mode of the alkene [35], probably arising from the loaded CDs. The two small but sharp peaks near 1462 and 1350 cm−1 correspond to the SO stretching or CO bands of sulfate, possibly from the oxidized form of thiourea or acetate [36]. Notably, the C–N stretching mode of aromatic amines, indicated by a peak at approximately 1280 cm−1 only appears in the profiles of ZnS-en and ZnS-trien, but not in those of ZnS-none or ZnS-EDTA. Therefore, C loaded on ZnS-en and ZnS-trien would exist as N-doped CDs. The two sharp peaks at approximately 1100 and 1050 cm−1 correspond to the C–O stretching mode derived from acetate and the SO stretching mode derived from sulfoxide [37]. The latter peak is particularly sharp because this band is the ZnS lattice resulting from the resonance between the vibrational modes of sulfide ions in the crystal [38]. The peaks at approximately 890, 800, and 659 cm−1, attributed to the C–S group of thiourea, are observed in all samples [39]. The distinct absorption band in the range of 450–650 cm−1 is related to the vibrational properties of ZnS [40]. ZnS-none and ZnS-EDTA show sharper FTIR peaks because the surfaces of these two samples have fewer CDs. Fig. 3 b shows the Raman spectra of the four particle types, measured using a 750 nm light source. Cubic zinc blende ZnS belongs to the point group Td (43 m) with two atoms per basic unit cell, and it is known to have three photo-phonons with T2 = 15 symmetry [41]. The doubly degenerate TO and higher-frequency single LO phonons are easily observable with first-order Raman scattering. In general, the bands of pure zinc blende ZnS measured for T2 TO and T2 LO modes appear at positions of 278 and 351 cm−1 [42]. Meanwhile, wurtzite ZnS belongs to the C6v (6 mm) spatial group, and the region-centered photo-phonon in primitive cells can be classified by a reduced expression, i.e., A1 + E1 + 2E2 + 2B1, where B1 is a silent mode, A1 and E1 are polar Raman- and infrared-active mode, and E2 is non-polar and only Raman-active. Therefore, three bands corresponding to the A1 TO, E1 TO, and E2 modes can be observed at 275, 279, and 285 cm−1 [43]. In all particles, herein, an LA mode corresponding to lattice vibrations in the crystals sharply appears at 200 cm−1, and two peaks at 278 and 351 cm−1 are observed, which are attributed to the cubic zinc blende ZnS crystal structure. Therefore, the ZnS crystals obtained here have a nearly cubic structure mixed with some wurtzite structure. The vibration mode corresponding to υ(S–S) at approximately 430–550 cm−1 indicates non-uniform S-defects in the particles. A υ(C–S) aliphatic vibration is observed at approximately 630–790 cm−1; this peak is attributed to thiourea. In general, the graphite G band, representing crystalline carbon, can be observed at 1400–1600 cm−1 and the D band, representing defects, appears at 1200–1300 cm−1 [44]. In ZnS-none, no G band is observed for the loaded CDs, which may be amorphous. However, in the ZnS samples with capping agents, small D bands are observed, suggesting some crystallinity. The D bands are especially clear in the spectra of ZnS-en and ZnS-trien, indicating that the C loaded on these ZnS particles is somewhat crystalline. Delocalization of electron density is not a prerogative of crystalline structure, but lays the specific property of graphene as well [45]. The two bands at the extra 1080–110 cm−1 and 1610–1680 cm−1 observed in ZnS-trien are probably due to the delocalization of electron density by C–N, indicating the existence of various carbon types. DSC/thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is a technique used to evaluate the thermal stability of a material. To determine the crystallinity of the carbon loaded on the ZnS particles, DSC/TGA curves monitoring the weight loss in the atmosphere were obtained for the synthesized ZnS particles, as shown in Fig. 3 c (Figs S6b and S6c). DSC peaks are generated by thermal transitions, including melting, oxidation, and chemical reactions, within the sample. Three main DSC peaks are observed [46]. The first is an endothermic peak at approximately 150 °C caused by dehydration. The second is a broad exothermic peak at approximately 300–500 °C caused by the crystallization of an amorphous CD into graphite. This corresponds to the removal of the organic impurities on the crystal surface observed by FT-IR. Finally, the third is a sharp exothermic peak that occurs at approximately 550–680 °C, mainly caused by the phase transform of ZnS to ZnO; the loaded CDs also undergo combustion at this temperature. In ZnS-none, a weight loss corresponding to approximately 1.5% dehydration appeared as an endothermic peak, and a broad exothermic peak corresponding to the CD crystallization, with a weight loss of 1.5%, indicating removed organic impurities, was observed. Finally, an exothermic peak in which ZnS was converted to ZnO in air appeared with a weight loss of 13% at approximately 620 °C. Calculated by molecular weight, the theoretical weight loss should be equivalent to 16%. No weight loss by dehydration occurred in ZnS-en. At 300–550 °C, a relatively significant exothermic peak corresponding to CD crystallization was observed, but the weight loss was small. It can be expected that CD crystallization predominates over the thermal decomposition of surface-adsorbed impurities. ZnS-en was converted to ZnO by air at 610 °C, which was lower than the transition temperature of ZnS-none, and the weight loss was 15% greater. However, because the DSC exothermic peak is broad and phase transition ends at a temperature of 700 °C or higher, it can be concluded that it is more difficult to expose ZnS-en particles to air than ZnS-none particles because ZnS-en are covered with CDs. In the TGA curve of ZnS-trien, the weight loss due to the thermal decomposition of surface organic impurities was 2% or more, probably due to the carbon shell enclosing the surface. Because the weight loss of Zn-EDTA is very small up to 600 °C, it is speculated that very few organic impurities exist on the surface. The majority of the carbon supported on the ZnS particles synthesized here is amorphous with some graphite. Many articles have already reported that the heat treatment of acids with various sulfur-, oxygen-, and amine-containing bases produces carbon polymer dots (CPDs). Schneider et al. used three different amines in citric acid to obtain CPDs. They found that the CPDs in the reaction of citric acid and hexamethylenetetramine have three to five derivative molecular states, whereas CPDs generated during that of citric acid and triethanolamine have amorphous structures and many surface groups whose molecular states cannot be determined [47]. Fig. 3 d shows the EDS patterns of the four types of ZnS particles, and the composition ratios of Zn and S for each type are shown in the table below. The Zn content is significantly lower than that of S in most particles. The atomic ratios of Zn:S in ZnS-non, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA were 0.9:1.0, 0.92:1.0, 0.90:1.0, and 0.97:1.0, respectively. It can be observed that the samples were up to 11% deficient in Zn. ZnS-none had the most Zn deficiency, while ZnS-EDTA had the least.

To confirm the surface chemical compositions of the particles and the oxidation states of the constituent elements, XPS profiles of the four ZnS particle types were obtained, as shown in Fig. 4. The high-resolution XPS profile of Zn 2p for ZnS-none is separated into two peaks at 1021.5 and 1044.6 eV with an interval of about 23.1 eV; these are attributed to Zn 2p 3/2 and Zn 2p 1/2, respectively [48]. For ZnS-en, these peaks are slightly shifted to lower binding energies. The degree of migration is increased as the chain length of the capping agent increases; however, the peaks return to high binding energies in the spectrum of ZnS-EDTA. The shift to lower binding energy indicates the reduction of Zn ions, showing that electrons are shifted toward Zn with the addition of en. This may be caused by the electron-donating character of the CDs doped on the ZnS surface, the doping of anions that provide more electrons than S does to the Zn2+ ions, or the electron-receiving Zn cation being insufficient compared to the electron-donating S anion. However, as the doped N and O have higher electronegativity than S, they attract more electrons; this is because of the electron-donating effect of CDs or the Zn deficiency. Typically, the S 2p orbital in ZnS is deconvoluted into peaks at 161.7 and 162.9 eV, assigned to S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1/2 of S2−, respectively [49]. The binding energies at 160.6 and 163.8 eV are also attributed to the bridging S2 2− [50]. These peaks suggest the presence of unsaturated S atoms on the ZnS surface, which eventually contribute to VS in the lattice. In the ZnS particles with capping agents, the S 2p peaks are shifted to lower binding energies; the degree of this shift is greater in ZnS-trien, which has a longer en chain length. Some reports state that VZn increases the electron density around S2– [51]. Therefore, it is considered that VZn and VS were formed simultaneously in the capped ZnS. However, in ZnS-EDTA, the electron donor is not N, but the O atom of the acetate-end group. O does not donate electrons well, and thus does not increase the electron density of Zn. Therefore, it is speculated that EDTA does not significantly affect VS or VZn. It can be predicted that the capping agent, especially N-based en, simultaneously induces VZn and VS in the ZnS particles. In general, in ZnS, Zn2+ ions with small ionic radii occupy interstitial sites in the lattice, resulting in frequent Frenkel defects in which empty Zn sites are formed in the lattice. Frenkel defects are generally found in ionic crystals, where the anions are much larger than the cations; the ionic radius of S2− (170 pm) is approximately double that of Zn2+ (88 pm). However, the defects may be somewhat alleviated by the electron-donating effect of the CDs. Meanwhile, the presence of CC bonds is confirmed by C 1 s peaks in the range of 284.6–9 eV, and the peaks at 285.9–286.7 and 286.5–288.7 eV are mainly due to CO or C–N and N–CN bonds, respectively [52]. In ZnS-none, most of the carbon is CC, and the peak appears at 284.9 eV with a small doping amount. In capped ZnS, the peak is larger and broader. Except for ZnS-trien, most of the carbon corresponds to CC, thus it is considered that the carbon present in the ZnS particles is loaded as CDs on the ZnS crystal surfaces. However, N is included in the CDs on the ZnS-trien particles. The particle surface partially doped with CDs acts as a light absorber or an electron donor or acceptor, which effectively separate photoexcited charges. The N 1 s signal at 410.6 eV in ZnS-none is due to the presence of S–Zn–N lattice bonds, indicating that N atoms are doped in the ZnS lattice [53], but the amount is negligibly small. In the ZnS particles with capping agents, this peak is very large and shifted to a lower binding energy, as the peak corresponding to S 2p. This means that the amount of N doping in the ZnS particles is increased; however, N does not strongly donate electrons to Zn. Regardless, N doping of the lattice can change the valence band (VB) position in the crystal and act as a hole trap to move photoexcited charges. Finally, an O 1 s peak is observed at 531.58 eV, which is attributed to M–O [54]. In the ZnS-none and ZnS-EDTA particles, this peak is large, and a peak corresponding to M–OH is also observed. This means that, without a capping agent, the solvent O is doped and the probability of the coexistence of ZnS and ZnO is high; the same result is obtained when using a capping agent with O as an electron donor. However, in the ZnS-en particles, no peak corresponding to M–OH appears, and the area of the peak corresponding to M–O is small. Additionally, it is speculated that the doped O is bonded weakly to Zn because the peak shifts to a lower binding energy. The reduction of the S and Zn ions by the introduction of the en capping agent indicates that the ZnS lattice is deficient in both ions. In other words, VS were formed as small-radius N and O were doped by the introduction of the en capping agent, and small-radius Zn ion defects remain in the ZnS lattice. However, the N and O doped in ZnS are conjectured to bond weakly with Zn because the CD doped outside the lattice stoichiometrically compensates for the charge by pushing or pulling electrons.

Fig. S7 shows the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the ZnS particles. The isotherm of ZnS-none is type I without voids in the IUPAC series; the other ZnS particles showed typical type IV curves that appear in mesoporous materials [55]. The specific surface areas of ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA were 3.8, 34.0, 14.2, and 14.9 m2 g−1, respectively; the pore volume followed the same trend. The pore radius was the largest for ZnS-en and the smallest for ZnS-EDTA. The high surface area and pore volume of ZnS-en are expected to provide a suitable environment for the transport of reactants in photocatalytic reactions and thereby enhance the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band energy states in defective ZnS particles
ce:section.ce:para: The DRS-UV–visible absorbance spectra of the ZnS particles are shown in Fig. 5 a. Unlike ZnS-none, whose absorption peak is observed at the bottom of 400 nm, the absorption does not reach zero for any capped ZnS until 700 nm. This may be caused by structural defects, but it is speculated that the conduction band (CB) is compensated by the doped CDs loaded on the crystals, resulting in a slightly narrow bandgap or baseline excitation. The absorption peaks were converted to a Tauc plot, as shown in Fig. 5 b, to obtain the bandgap. Because ZnS is a direct semiconductor, the bandgap was determined by finding the energy that met the x-axis by extrapolating the tangent line. The calculated bandgaps for ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA were 3.25, 3.29, 3.21, and 3.25 eV, respectively. From the Mott–Schottky plot in Fig. 5 c, it was confirmed that all of the synthesized ZnS particles were n-type semiconductors. In the XPS-valence band curve of Fig. 5 d, the valence band maximum (VBM) of ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA were 1.62, 1.52, 1.30, and 1.37 eV, respectively. By subtracting the bandgap values obtained in Fig. 5 b from these values, the positions of the CB minimum (CBM) are determined to be − 1.63, − 1.77, − 1.91, and − 1.63 eV, respectively. From these results, we can draw band potential energy diagrams for the four particle types as shown in Fig. 5 e. ZnS-none has a significantly reduced bandgap compared to that of pure non-defective ZnS (3.6 eV); the VBM energy level is moved to upward by Vs and O-doping. In the ZnS particles with capping agents, the bandgaps are slightly larger than those of ZnS-none, but still significantly reduced compared to those of pure ZnS. Moreover, in ZnS-en and ZnS-trien with high O- and N-doping, the shift of VBM is slightly larger than in ZnS-none. The reason for the lower than expected rise may be due to the opposite effect caused by VZn. The CBM energy level is located somewhat higher in ZnS-en and ZnS-trien than in ZnS-none. Although there were many VZn, the CDs doping was extensive, causing a slight rise in the CBM location by complementing each other. Voigt et al. showed that the electronic properties of nanocomposites of surface-defect-heavy nanoparticles with functionalized carbon could be advantageously tuned using the synergistic effect generated from the induced electric field of the structure [56]. They revealed that ZnS nanoparticles synthesized with Zn/S> 1 having Zn-rich (S-deficient) surfaces could show a CBM shift to 0.7 eV by bonding with carbon, which is in agreement with our results. Despite these various defects, the energy diagrams of all ZnS particles synthesized still contain the oxidation–reduction potential for water-splitting.

In order to theoretically confirm the Zn or S defects, DOS calculations were performed at the interface where the cubic and wurtzite ZnS structures were coexisted. Many researchers have calculated that the formation of VS is more energetically favorable than VZn [57]. Thus, in order to simulate defects at the interface where two crystals coexist, the crystal was optimized by removing random S atoms from the interface as shown in Fig. S8a. DOS calculations confirmed that the band gaps of cubic ZnS and wurtzite ZnS were 3.54 eV and 3.38 eV, respectively (Fig. S8b). This is a higher value compared to the experimental value of 3.21–3.29 eV, probably because that the carbon-doping was excluded. On the other hand, as Vs are formed at the interface, a new energy state in which the VB and CB levels are narrowed appears, which is directly related to the reduction of the bandgap. In other words, as one new energy band is created above the original VB band, the VB level is moved upward, resulting in a lowered bandgap. This is believed to correspond to an additional energy state caused by strong coupling between ionized charges and carriers due to Vs [58]. On the other hand, the CB level was also slightly lowered, but no new band state was formed in there. In the end, from theoretical calculations, it is confirmed that Vs at the interface of ZnS where cubic and wurtzite structures coexist contribute to raising the VB level upward while reducing the bandgap. Ghosh's research team also found that Zn vacancies in ZnS hardly change the energy states, but S vacancies form new energy states [59]. On the other hand, several researchers reported that the band position of graphene, which was 0 V in the structure where ZnS and graphene were bonded, shifted to the upward by about 1.0 V or more after bonding through DOS calculations [60]. Their results also support that the CB energy level shifts upward in ZnS-en or ZnS-trien in the energy diagram of Fig. 5 e.

Crystal defects affect the PL properties of nanocrystals by generating intermediate energy levels in the bandgap. To verify the presence of VZn or VS in each particle, PL spectra of ZnS solid powders were obtained using an emission wavelength of 290 nm, and the results are presented in Fig. 6 a. An intrinsic emission peak related to the bandgap of ZnS appears at 400 nm. The two peaks at 450 and 465 nm are attributed to the structural defects in ZnS. Some researchers attribute the former and latter peak to VS and VZn, respectively [61]. Therefore, the PL curve indicates that ZnS-none contains many VZn but a few VS. In ZnS-en and ZnS-trien, the amount of VS is increased. Meanwhile, ZnS-EDTA contains excessive S defects. These results are consistent with our XPS results. The PL intensity is related to the degree of photoexcited charge recombination and catalytic activity [62]. When the intensity of the PL curve decreases, charge recombination decreases, and consequently catalytic activity increases. Herein, the PL intensity decreased in the order of ZnS-none, ZnS-EDTA, ZnS-en, and ZnS-trien. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 6 b, the decay rates of the PL curve with time were 38.78, 40.54, and 49.13 ns for ZnS-none, ZnS-en, and ZnS-trien particles, respectively. This suggests that ZnS-trien would have the best photocatalytic performance because the PL decay rate was the lowest. In the photoreaction system, energy transfer is considered as important as electron transfer. One useful tool for elucidating the energy transfer of carbon derivatives is the observation of fluorescence quenching. When the donor is placed close enough to the acceptor and the two particles combine through an electrostatic interaction, energy flows from the donor to the acceptor. Carbon derivatives composed of sp2 carbon are unique energy acceptors, absorbing energy across the entire visible spectrum very uniformly and efficiently without exhibiting any fluorescence. Mackowski and Kamińska revealed that the energy quenching of carbon derivatives is driven by dipole-dipole interactions as well as mechanisms involving light-induced oscillations of electrons in carbon derivatives [63]. In particular, they argued that electrons excited in graphene affect the extinction efficiency, and due to this property, the electronic and plasmonic properties of graphene at the interface of hybrid nanostructures can be controlled with light. Another research group also reported a 70-fold extinction of the fluorescence intensity in CdSe/ZnS nanocrystals placed on graphene [64]. Moreover, the quenching efficiency increased as the number of graphene layers increased. However, in time-resolved PL of this study, the decay time was delayed in ZnS-en or ZnS-trien containing CDs shell than in ZnS-none without CDs shell. This indicates no quenching by CDs shell. Consequently, this means that the charge transfers between ZnS and CDs shell works more effectively by electron transfer than by energy transfer in this study. As shown in Fig. 6 c, the intensity of photocurrent density increased in the order of ZnS-none, ZnS-EDTA, ZnS-en, and ZnS-trien. The photocurrent density under visible light irradiation was the highest and most stable in ZnS-trien, meaning that ZnS-trien exhibits better visible light response and more efficient charge separation than the other samples. The time-dependent decay rate of the photocurrent density, shown in Fig. 6 d, remains stable for all particles up to 330 s. However, a magnification of this graph shows that the photocurrent density increases somewhat from 150 s in ZnS-trien and ZnS-en (particularly in ZnS-en), whereas it decreases in ZnS-none and ZnS-EDTA (particularly in ZnS-EDTA). Maintaining the photocurrent for a long time means that the excited charges continue to flow without recombination, which means that the charge transfer distance is long. The small increase in the photocurrent density from 150 s is a clue that there is a factor that helping charge transfer in the particle. Conversely, a slight decrease in photocurrent means that there exists a factor that gradually reduces charge transfer within the particle. Presumably, in ZnS-trien and ZnS-en, appropriate VZn and VS effectively separate photoexcited charges and increase the photocurrent density. Moreover, the carbon shell doped on the ZnS also has a positive effect. Meanwhile, in ZnS-none and ZnS-EDTA, excess VZn or VS would have acted as a recombination center for photoexcited charges, resulting in a gradual decrease in photocurrent density over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2 generation performance by CO2 conversion and water-splitting on ZnS particles and durability evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: H2 production performance by water-splitting catalyzed by the ZnS particles was performed using pure distilled water with no sacrificial reagent under one-sun conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the amounts of H2 generated from ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA over 10 h reaction were 1775, 2547, 984.6, and 134.4 μmol g−1, respectively. The water-splitting performance of ZnS-trien was poorer than expected; this sample was expected to have the most effective photoinduced charge separation because it showed the highest photocurrent density and lowest PL intensity. The discrepancy is related to the activation energy for water adsorption–desorption to the particles, as presented in Fig. S9. The activation energies of water desorption for ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA were 38.25, 43.86, 31.06, and 21.26 kJ mol−1, respectively, as calculated by Ozawa’s equation. These values indicate that water is most strongly adsorbed to the ZnS-en surface. To achieve advantageous water splitting, water must first be strongly adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface; when water is well adsorbed on the catalyst as a reactant, H2 production increases. However, because the surface of ZnS-trien was covered by CD, water adsorption was hindered. Therefore, despite the excellent optical properties, water splitting was suppressed and less H2 was generated. Fig. S10 and Table S2 show the results of measuring the surface potential of the catalysts in aqueous solution and the contact angle for water droplets. The surface potential is a result measured immediately after ultrasonic treatment for 1 min after adding the sample to the aqueous solution. ZnS-none showed a rather stable surface potential value of + 12.63 mV, and the surface potential value decreased as the number of carbon chains present on the surface increased, such as ZnS-en and ZnS-trien. This is considered to be because the CDs-covered catalysts, ZnS-en or ZnS-trien, are hydrophobic, so they do not mix well with polar solvent, water, resulted the degree of dispersion is lowered. Meanwhile, the surface contact angle was measured by processing powdered particles into a film. As a value measured immediately after 24 h of storage in a dark room, the angle was the highest as 48.7° in ZnS-trien, which has a lot of carbon on the crystal surface. This means that the surface is very hydrophobic. On the other hand, the contact angle of the ZnS-none catalyst without CDs shell was the lowest at 13.2°, which the surface was somewhat hydrophilic properties. Fig. 7 b presents a summary of the products obtained from the CO2 reduction reaction for 10 h under one-sun conditions in each particle. Fig. S11a shows the amount of CH4 and CO produced during the CO2 reduction reaction at intervals of 1 h. Prior to comparing the activities, it was investigated whether the carbon source of the produced carbon compound was pure CO2 injected into the reactor or was caused by the decomposition of carbon impurities attached to the catalyst or reactor surface. Using 13CO2 (99 atom-% 13 C, 99.93 atom-% 16O, Sigma-Adrich, USA) as a reactant, the detected product was analyzed by GC-Mass. At this time, it is very important to perform a blank experiment when nothing is supplied to the reactor and only the catalyst is present. No peaks were detected in the blank test in this study, so the catalyst is considered very clean. For the 13CO2 reduction, all reaction conditions were carried out under exactly the same experimental conditions as the actual CO2 reduction experiment (e.g. light intensity, catalytic amount) under argon gas/light on. As shown in Fig. S12, 12CO2 was not detected at all in the 13CO2 reduction reaction after 1 h of reaction. This means that the carbon compounds produced were produced exclusively by externally injected 13CO2 reduction. On the other hand, the reaction intermediates resulting from the 13CO2 (m/z = 45) reduction reaction are m/z = 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, and 29, which are the isotopic carbon compounds 13C, 13CH,13CH2, 13CH3, 13CH4, and 13CO. In general, the photoreduction reaction from CO2 to CH4 is a very complex process involving the transfer of 8-electrons and 8-protons, breaking of C-O bonds, and formation of C-H bonds. There are two recommended pathways here, one is the formaldehyde pathway (fast hydrogenation pathway) where the reaction follows the pathway CO2 → HCOOH → HCHO → CH3OH → CH4 [65]. H13COOH and 13CH3OH were not observed in the GC-MS study in this study. The second is the carbene pathway (fast deoxygenation pathway) where CO2 is reduced along the pathway CO2 → CO → C or CHO → CH2 → CH3 → CH4 [66]. From the observation of 13C, 13CH2, and 13CH3 intermediates in GC-MS analysis, this pathway is likely to dominate in this study. The majority of the product of CO2 reduction was CO, which is a primary reduction product, rather than CH4, and the production ratio of CH4:CO was about 1:6. The combined CO2 photoconversion efficiency of CH4 and CO was 0.13, 1.11, 1.89, and 1.01 μmol g−1 for ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA, respectively; ZnS-trien exhibited the best performance, which is in good agreement with the results of optical characterization. The CO2 conversion efficiency is also closely related to the adsorption capacity of CO2 as a reactant. Fig. S11b shows the amount of CO2 adsorption for each particle. The CO2 adsorption capacity increased significantly in the order of ZnS-none, ZnS-EDTA, ZnS-en, and ZnS-trien, which was consistent with the CO2 light conversion efficiency. This order confirmed that the water-splitting performance was the best in ZnS-en, while the CO2 photoreduction performance was the best in ZnS-trien. It is conjectured that the photoinduced charge-separation efficiency was improved by the appropriate levels of VZn and VS in these two particles, and the CDs loading facilitated the absorption of light to increase the photoactivation rate and amplify the photoexcited charge production [67]. With ZnS-trien, the CO2 photoreduction performance was more than 14 times greater than that with ZnS-none; with ZnS-en particles, the H2 generation efficiency was increased by 1.43 times relative to that with ZnS-none. These results are very encouraging, as there is no case of producing 254.7 μmol g-1 h-1 of H2 by decomposing pure water without a sacrificial agent, such as a photocatalyst, particularly when using a single heterojunction-free photocatalyst. This suggests that harmonious Zn and S co-defects and C, N, O co-doping are more effective for increasing the photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation. The durability of ZnS-en, which has the best water-splitting performance, was confirmed through repeated recycling tests. As shown in Fig. 7 c, one cycle was 10 h, which was repeated five times. The amount of H2 generated from the ZnS-en particles was maintained through the second cycle, but it decreased gradually from the third cycle, and in the fifth cycle, it was 2351 μmol g−1, which is a decrease of about 7.8% compared to the initial amount. This trend indicates that the ZnS-en particles were slightly corroded as the number of cycles increased during the photoreaction. Recycling tests were also conducted in the CO2 photoreduction reaction. ZnS-trien, which is the best catalyst, showed excellent and stable performance over five cycles. This is attributed to the VZn or N, O co-doping raising the VB position, which weakens the oxidation ability of the photoinduced holes, thereby protecting the ZnS-trien particles from photocorrosion [68].

Measuring the apparent quantum yield (AQY) of the photocatalytic reaction by combining the diffuse reflectance spectrum and the reaction rate is very useful for understanding the photochemical reaction at the solid-gas interface. In addition to this, information on internal and external quantum efficiencies is also quite useful in photoelectrochemical research. In particular, the external quantum efficiency (EQE) is the photocurrent collected per incident photon to current efficiency, i.e. the incident photon flux as a function of the irradiation wavelength. EQE is presented in Fig. S13, and the AQY that measured using a 100 W Xe-lamp equipped with a 360 nm ( ± 5 nm) band pass cut filter is shown in Table S3. EQE values showed constant and stable values in all samples in the wavelength range of 330–370 nm. At 360 nm wavelength, the ZnS-trien catalyst showed the highest 5.23%. ZnS-none, ZnS-en, and ZnS-EDTA were 3.61%, 4.86%, and 4.12%, respectively, suggesting that the presence of CDs shell affects the quantum efficiency. On the other hand, the AQY values of ZnS-none, ZnS-en, ZnS-trien, and ZnS-EDTA were 3.16%, 4.56%, 1.74%, and 0.23% at 360 nm ( ± 5 nm), respectively, and it was the highest in ZnS-en. In contrast, ZnS-trien exhibited relatively low AQY due to low hydrogen generation.

In catalytic reactions, it is a very important step to confirm changes in the crystal structure and oxidation state of a catalyst after a long-term reaction. XRD and XPS were measured for the two catalysts, ZnS-en and ZnS-trien, after 50 h-reaction, and the results are presented in Fig. S14. First, in the XRD (S14a and S14c) before and after 50 h-reaction, both samples show the same pattern and intensity as the before reaction without shift or decrease in peaks. This is evidence that both samples are structurally very stable even for long-term photoreactions. Moreover, the elution of S, which was frequently seen in metal sulfide-based catalysts, did not appear at all in the two ZnS. XPS (S14b and S14d) also showed the same results, with a slight difference being that the peaks were slightly broadened after the reaction. In general, an extension of the full width at half maximum (FWHM) can occur due to changes in the number of chemical bond coordination, changes in sample conditions, or differential charges on the surface [69]. Perhaps, the broadening of the peak after the reaction is thought to be caused by the adsorptions of H+ ions onto ZnS during the photoreaction (change of coordination number due to surface adsorption). In particular, the broadening of the N 1 s or O 1 s peaks in ZnS-en was due to N-H or O-H formed by the adsorption of H+. The S 2p and C 1 s peaks in ZnS-trien were also slightly broadened, which are also expected to be due to the generation of S-H or C-H due to H+ adsorption. However, since there is no shift in the peak position, it is unreasonable to judge that the oxidation state or structure has changed.

To estimate the charge-transfer path on the surface, spin-trapping ESR analysis using DMPO as a free radical trapping agent was performed to detect ∙OH and ∙O2 − radicals over time (0, 5, 10, and 20 min), as shown in Figs. S15a and b, respectively. After adding 40 mL of DMPO to an aqueous solution of 1.0 mL of H2O2 and 9.0 mL of H2O, 10.0 mg of a ZnS sample was dispersed to measure ∙OH radicals. The same amount of DMPO was added to 10 mL of CH3OH and used to disperse 10.0 mg of a ZnS sample to measure ∙O2 − radicals. Fig. 8 summarizes the results after 20 min under one-sun conditions. With irradiation, a small signal corresponding to the DMPO-∙OH radical is observed (Fig. 8 a); which increases in all three samples with increasing irradiation time. This increase indicates that when electrons present in the VB of ZnS are excited to the CB under visible light, ∙OH radicals are generated in the VB holes, and the amount of radical generation increases with time. The DMPO-∙OH signal according to the light irradiation time was greater in ZnS-en and ZnS-trien than in ZnS-none. In particular, the intensity of the signal in ZnS-en after 20 min was slightly higher than that in ZnS-trien, indicating that the amount of ∙OH radicals generated in the VB of ZnS-en is higher than that generated in other ZnS particles. The CDs loaded on ZnS-en may act as electron sinks, attracting electrons from the CB of ZnS-en and exciting more electrons from the VB. This effect of CD produced more H+ ions and ∙OH radicals in the VB of ZnS-en, eventually increasing H2 generation. In Fig. 8 b and Fig. S15b, the DMPO-∙O2 − signal is observed more clearly. The signal intensity increases significantly in all three samples according to the light irradiation time. This trend indicates that many electrons excited from the VB of the ZnS particles were collected in the CB or on CD surfaces by light irradiation. The DMPO-∙O2 − signal appears to be stronger than that from DMPO-∙OH because the CDs loaded on ZnS attract electrons, promoting charge separation and increasing the charge-transfer rate. The DMPO-∙O2 − signal was slightly more significant for ZnS-trien than for ZnS-en, which is attributed to the N-CD shell on ZnS-trien that strongly attracts electrons from the CB. The strong electron-attracting effect of N-CD would promote the CO2 reduction performance of ZnS-trien. In conclusion, the N-CDs loaded on the ZnS surface enhanced the ∙O2 − radicals, while the CDs increased the number of ∙OH radicals generated in the VB of ZnS. Moreover, it seems that N, O co-doping and the proper levels of VZn and VS moved the bandgap to an appropriate position, ensuring stability against photocorrosion. The water-splitting performance and CO2 reduction performance are thereby significantly increased in ZnS-en and ZnS-trien, respectively. IMVS was performed, with results shown in Fig. S16, to determine the photoexcited charge recombination rate in ZnS particles. The recombination rates calculated from these curves are presented in Fig. 8 c. The IMVS semicircle is the widest for ZnS-trien; it is also shifted further to the left. A larger semicircle with leftward shifting in the IMVS curve indicates that the photoexcited charges are better separated, and the recombination is slower [70]. From these semicircular curves, recombination time (s, y-axis) was calculated using the equation s = 1/2πfr [71], where fr is the minimum value of the characteristic frequency of the hypothetical IMVS. The charge recombination rates obtained from the correlation between light intensity and time increased in the order of ZnS-none, ZnS-EDTA, ZnS-en, and ZnS-trien. As the light intensity increased, the charge recombination rate slightly increased for all particles. However, it was the lowest in ZnS-en. This result suggests excellent photoactivity in ZnS-trien owing to the lower photoexcited charge recombination rate, and implies stable photoactivity by ZnS-en, even under strong light.

Based on the above results, the mechanism of H2 production and CO2 photoreduction from water splitting catalyzed by ZnS-en and ZnS-trien under one-sun irradiation is predicted, as shown in Scheme 1. In the grains of ZnS-en and Zn-trien, N or O doping in the ZnS lattice and partial VZn occur simultaneously, and their VB positions are shifted to higher than those of pure ZnS. Strong CD or N-CD doping occurs, but is complemented by VS; the VB positions are higher than those of the CB of pure ZnS, but not significantly. The bandgaps of ZnS-en and Zn-trien are 3.29 and 3.21 eV, which are narrower than that of pure ZnS. The CDs and N-CDs doped on the ZnS-en and Zn-trien surfaces act as electron sinks [72]. First, during light-irradiated water splitting, the CDs loaded on the particle surface attract excited electrons from the VB of ZnS-en and become electron-rich pools. Water is adsorbed into the VB holes of ZnS-en as the electrons are excited, thus generating H+ ions and ∙OH radicals. The H+ ions are transported to the electron-rich CD pools, reduced, and converted to H2. Meanwhile, the N-CDs surrounding ZnS-trien could act as either photosensitizers that donate electrons or as electron sinks that attract electrons. Based on the generation of CH4 as a product of CO2 conversion, it is presumed that a water-splitting reaction occurs simultaneously. Considering the reaction potential, it is reasonable to conclude that N-CD acts as an electron sink rather than as a photosensitizer. Therefore, during an irradiated CO2 conversion reaction, electrons that are excited from the VB of ZnS-trien and collected in the CB move toward the N-CDs. In the resulting holes of the ZnS-trien VB, water splits to generate O2 and H+ ions. In the electron-rich N-CDs, the adsorbed CO2 receives electrons, reduces to CO, and then reacts with H+ that has been decomposed in the ZnS-trien holes to generate CH4. Ultimately, properly formed VZn and VS in ZnS-en and ZnS-trien act as hole and electron traps, respectively, to move photoinduced charges faster, thus favoring charge separation. Moreover, an appropriate VZn level increases the VB energy and greatly weakens the oxidation ability of the holes, thereby protecting the ZnS particles from photocorrosion and helping to maintain the photocatalytic activity for a long time. In conclusion, this study confirms that efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution or CO2 conversion can be attained when adequate VZn and VS, N and O doping, and strong C doping are introduced simultaneously.

Herein, we elucidated the relationship between defects and photocatalytic activity by synthesizing Zn- and S-defective ZnS particles using different capping agents and examining the optical properties. The defective ZnS crystals had mixed structures of cubic zinc blende and hexagonal wurtzite. The content and appearance of doped carbon varies according to the chain length of the capping agent and its derivatives. The addition of the en-derivative capping agents induced VZn and VS formation and N, O co-doping in the lattice, as well as crystalline CD shell loading on the ZnS surfaces. The degree of VZn and VS were predicted through PL, XPS, and DOS calculation. In particular, it was confirmed from DOS calculations that a new energy state appeared due to VS generated at the ZnS interface where cubic and wurtzite coexist, which had the effect of narrowing the bandgap. Slower charge recombination and higher photocurrent densities were observed in ZnS-en and ZnS-trien than in ZnS-none. Despite not using a sacrificial agent, the amount of H2 generated after 10 h using ZnS-en was 2547 μmol g−1, and the yield of CO2 conversion after 10 h using ZnS-trien was 14 times higher than that obtained using ZnS-none. Consequently, this study revealed that, in ZnS particles prepared with en-derivative capping agents, the changes in bandgap energy by the appropriate Zn and S defects and effective charge separation arising from CD shell doping promote the generation of ∙O2 − and ∙OH radicals. These results may serve as a driving force for formulating new strategies to develop stable and inexpensive photocatalysts in the future.

Hojoon Moon, Sujeong Kim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Minkyu Kim, No-Kuk Park, Joonwoo Kim: Investigation, Visualization, Software Jeom-In Baek, Ho-Jung Ryu: Formal analysis, Data curation Namgyu Son: Software, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Misook Kang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
